enrichment.short_description: Avatier is an enterprise identity governance and administration (IGA) platform that automates user provisioning, single sign-on, password management, and IT self-service through a containerized, workflow-driven architecture.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom quote-based pricing; contact sales (volume discounts for 3,000+ users and educational institutions)
enrichment.seo_title: Avatier Review 2026: Identity Management and IT Self-Service
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Avatier is an enterprise identity management platform offering SSO, password reset, and IT self-service automation. Read pricing, features, and 2026 review.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Avatier is an enterprise identity and access management (IAM) and identity governance and administration (IGA) software company founded in 1997 by Nelson Cicchitto, who continues to lead the company. Headquartered in Pleasanton, California, Avatier is a privately held C-corporation that has not disclosed external venture funding and reports serving over 500 customers worldwide, ranging from Global 100 enterprises to small businesses, with more than 7 million licenses sold historically. Its flagship product, Identity Anywhere, is a containerized (Docker-based) identity governance platform that bundles password management and self-service password reset, single sign-on (SSO) across cloud and on-premises applications, automated user lifecycle and provisioning workflows, an IT service catalog for self-service access requests, and access certification and governance reporting for compliance (SOX, HIPAA, and similar frameworks). Avatier markets itself as offering passwordless authentication options and AI-assisted access request and approval workflows layered onto its governance engine. The platform is aimed primarily at mid-market and large enterprise IT and security teams that need to reduce help desk password-reset volume, enforce least-privilege access, and pass access audits, rather than at individual consumers or very small businesses. Pricing is not published on Avatier's website; the company uses custom, quote-based pricing tied to user count and deployment model (cloud-hosted Identity Anywhere IDaaC or on-premises), with volume discounts available for organizations above roughly 3,000 users and for educational institutions. In the identity governance space, Avatier competes with larger, better-funded platforms such as SailPoint, Saviynt, Okta Identity Governance, and One Identity, as well as help-desk-focused password management tools; Avatier differentiates primarily on its self-service catalog metaphor, containerized deployment flexibility, and long operating history as an independent, privately held vendor rather than a venture-backed or recently acquired one. Because exact revenue, funding, and up-to-date customer counts are not publicly disclosed with precision, those figures should be treated as approximate and verified directly with Avatier during a sales evaluation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Avatier
paragraphs: Avatier is an identity governance and administration platform built to give enterprise IT and security teams centralized control over who has access to what, without turning every password reset or access request into a help desk ticket. Founded in 1997 and still privately held under founder Nelson Cicchitto, Avatier is one of the longer-tenured independent vendors in the identity management space.

The core product, Identity Anywhere, is delivered as a containerized platform that organizations can run in the cloud or on-premises. It combines password self-service, single sign-on, automated provisioning and deprovisioning, an internal IT service catalog, and access certification into one governance layer that sits in front of an organization's applications and directories.

Avatier positions the product for mid-size to large enterprises that need to reduce IT help desk load caused by password resets, enforce consistent access policies across cloud and legacy systems, and produce the access review evidence auditors expect for frameworks like SOX and HIPAA.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Avatier's feature set centers on four pillars: self-service, automation, governance, and single sign-on. Self-service password reset and account unlock let end users resolve common issues themselves through a branded portal or mobile app, cutting help desk call volume. A service catalog exposes application and group access as requestable items with configurable approval chains, so business users can request access without filing IT tickets manually.

On the governance side, Avatier provides access certification campaigns, entitlement reporting, and segregation-of-duties checks intended to help compliance and audit teams demonstrate least-privilege access. Universal workflow automation drives joiner-mover-leaver processes, automatically provisioning and revoking access as employees change roles or leave the organization.

Single sign-on and multi-factor authentication round out the platform, giving users one login experience across cloud SaaS applications and on-premises systems while giving security teams centralized authentication policy control. The Docker-based Identity Anywhere architecture is also marketed as making deployment and upgrades faster than legacy, monolithic IGA suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Avatier does not publish list pricing on its website. Instead, pricing is quote-based and depends on factors such as total user count, whether the deployment is hosted by Avatier (Identity Anywhere IDaaC) or run on-premises, and which modules (SSO, governance, service catalog) an organization licenses.

Avatier has indicated that organizations with more than roughly 3,000 users, as well as educational institutions, may qualify for volume or minimum-contract discount pricing. Prospective buyers should expect a sales consultation and formal quote rather than self-service checkout, which is typical for enterprise IGA software in this category.

features.title: Self-service password reset
features.description: Lets end users reset forgotten passwords and unlock accounts themselves through a branded portal or mobile app, reducing help desk tickets.

features.title: IT service catalog
features.description: Presents applications, groups, and access rights as requestable catalog items with configurable, multi-step approval workflows.

features.title: Single sign-on (SSO)
features.description: Provides one login across cloud SaaS applications and on-premises systems, with multi-factor authentication support.

features.title: Automated user lifecycle management
features.description: Automates joiner-mover-leaver provisioning and deprovisioning so access changes as employees change roles or leave the company.

features.title: Access governance and certification
features.description: Runs periodic access review campaigns and entitlement reports to support compliance audits such as SOX and HIPAA.

features.title: Segregation-of-duties controls
features.description: Flags conflicting access combinations to help prevent policy violations tied to toxic access combinations.

features.title: Containerized (Docker) deployment
features.description: Runs Identity Anywhere as a set of Docker containers, supporting cloud-hosted or on-premises deployment models.

features.title: Passwordless authentication options
features.description: Supports passwordless sign-in methods as an alternative to traditional password-based login.

pros: Long operating history (founded 1997) as an independent, privately held IGA vendor
Combines self-service, SSO, and governance in a single platform rather than requiring separate tools
Containerized architecture offers deployment flexibility between cloud and on-premises
Service catalog model is approachable for non-technical business users requesting access
Reports serving both large (Global 100) and small business customers
cons: No public pricing; every evaluation requires a sales quote and consultation
Smaller company footprint and marketing presence compared to category leaders like SailPoint and Okta
Detailed, independent third-party review volume is limited compared to larger competitors
On-premises and containerized deployment options may require more internal IT expertise than a pure SaaS competitor

faqs.question: What does Avatier do?
faqs.answer: Avatier is an identity governance and administration platform that automates password management, single sign-on, access requests, and access certification for enterprise IT teams.

faqs.question: How much does Avatier cost?
faqs.answer: Avatier does not publish list pricing. Pricing is custom and quote-based, depending on user count, deployment model, and licensed modules; contact Avatier sales for a quote.

faqs.question: Is Avatier cloud-based or on-premises?
faqs.answer: Both. Avatier's Identity Anywhere platform can be deployed as a cloud-hosted service (IDaaC) or run on-premises using its Docker container architecture.

faqs.question: Who founded Avatier and when?
faqs.answer: Avatier was founded in 1997 by Nelson Cicchitto, who remains involved with the company; it is a privately held C-corporation headquartered in Pleasanton, California.

faqs.question: What is Avatier Identity Anywhere?
faqs.answer: Identity Anywhere is Avatier's flagship product, bundling password self-service, single sign-on, provisioning automation, a service catalog, and access governance into one platform.

faqs.question: Who is Avatier's target customer?
faqs.answer: Avatier primarily targets mid-market and large enterprise IT and security teams that need to reduce help desk load and pass access compliance audits.

faqs.question: How does Avatier compare to SailPoint or Okta?
faqs.answer: Avatier competes in the same identity governance category as SailPoint, Saviynt, and Okta Identity Governance, but is a smaller, privately held vendor that differentiates on its self-service catalog approach and deployment flexibility rather than market scale.

faqs.question: Does Avatier support multi-factor authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, Avatier's single sign-on module includes multi-factor authentication support alongside passwordless login options.

use_cases.title: Reducing help desk password reset volume
use_cases.description: IT teams deploy Avatier's self-service portal so employees can reset passwords and unlock accounts without calling the help desk.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT support and help desk teams

use_cases.title: Automating employee access lifecycle
use_cases.description: HR and IT integrate Avatier's workflow automation so access is granted and revoked automatically as employees join, change roles, or leave.
use_cases.audience: IT operations and HR-IT integration teams

use_cases.title: Passing access compliance audits
use_cases.description: Compliance and security teams use Avatier's certification campaigns and entitlement reports to demonstrate least-privilege access for SOX, HIPAA, or similar audits.

enrichment.short_description: Avo is a data governance and tracking-plan management platform that helps product, engineering, and data teams design, validate, and enforce clean analytics event schemas before they reach production.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (2 editors); Team plan from $250/month billed annually
enrichment.headquarters: Walnut, California, USA (with an office in Reykjavik, Iceland)
enrichment.seo_title: Avo Review 2026: Analytics Tracking Plan and Data Governance
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Avo helps teams design and enforce clean analytics tracking plans. See Avo's features, pricing plans, and how it compares to spreadsheets in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Avo is a data governance and product-analytics tracking-plan management platform founded in 2018 by Stefania Olafsdottir (CEO), Solvi Logason, and Arni Hermann Reynisson, who previously worked together at the gaming company QuizUp. The company went through Y Combinator's Winter 2019 batch and has raised approximately 3.15 million dollars in total funding, including a 3 million dollar seed round in September 2020 led by GGV Capital with participation from Heavybit. Avo is headquartered in Walnut, California, with an additional office in Reykjavik, Iceland, and reports roughly 30 to 40 employees. The product replaces spreadsheet-based analytics tracking plans with a collaborative web interface where product managers, engineers, and data teams jointly define event and property schemas, naming conventions, and data types for product analytics. Its Codegen feature generates type-safe tracking libraries for iOS, Android, web, and backend languages so implementation matches the agreed schema, functioning like a compile-time spell-checker for analytics code. Its Inspector feature observes real production event data and flags discrepancies against the tracking plan, giving ongoing data-quality monitoring after launch, and its schema sync capability pushes validated schemas into downstream systems such as data warehouses or a company's own schema registry. Avo integrates with common analytics and customer data platforms including Segment, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Google Analytics, mParticle, and Rudderstack. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free plan includes two editor seats, unlimited comment-only seats, and a capped number of events observed by Inspector; a Team plan starts at 250 dollars per month billed annually (300 dollars billed monthly) for five editors, with additional editors priced at roughly 50 to 60 dollars per month each; and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with SSO, premium support, and enforceable governance policies. Avo's target audience is data-driven product and growth teams, typically at Series A through large enterprise companies, who rely on clean, trustworthy analytics data to make product decisions. It competes with general data governance and catalog tools as well as analytics-adjacent solutions like Segment Protocols and RudderStack Tracking Plans, differentiating itself through its dedicated, non-engineer-friendly tracking plan UI combined with developer-facing code generation and production-data inspection in one product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Avo
paragraphs: Avo is a data governance platform purpose-built for product analytics tracking plans. Instead of teams managing event schemas in spreadsheets that quickly go stale, Avo gives product managers, engineers, and data analysts a shared, collaborative interface to define what events, properties, and data types get tracked across a company's apps and websites.

Founded in 2018 by former QuizUp product leaders and backed by GGV Capital and Y Combinator, Avo has grown into a tool used by data-driven product teams to prevent the broken, inconsistent event data that commonly undermines analytics dashboards and A/B testing.

The platform's core idea is to catch analytics data-quality problems before they ship, using design-time validation, generated tracking code, and post-launch monitoring, rather than discovering bad data weeks later in a dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Avo's tracking plan editor is a collaborative workspace where teams define events, event properties, naming conventions, and value types, with branching and approval workflows similar to a code review process for analytics changes. Stakeholders across the company can comment and review changes without needing edit access.

Codegen turns the approved tracking plan into strongly typed tracking libraries for platforms like iOS, Android, web (JavaScript/TypeScript), and backend languages, so developers get autocomplete and compile-time errors if they try to send an event that does not match the design, rather than finding out after release.

Inspector observes live production analytics traffic and compares it against the tracking plan, surfacing missing events, unexpected properties, or type mismatches so teams can catch regressions quickly. Schema sync pushes validated schemas downstream into data warehouses or a company's own schema registry, and Avo integrates with tools such as Segment, Amplitude, Mixpanel, and Rudderstack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Avo offers a free plan with two editor seats, unlimited comment-only collaborators, five stakeholder domains, and up to 100,000 events observed per month in Inspector, aimed at small teams moving off spreadsheets.

The Team plan starts at 250 dollars per month when billed annually (300 dollars per month billed monthly) and includes five editor seats, approval workflows, branch audits, and a higher Inspector volume; additional editor seats cost roughly 50 dollars per month annually or 60 dollars per month on monthly billing.

The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds enforceable data design standards, automated required reviews, SSO, premium support, up to 1 million events observed in Inspector, and schema sync across an organization's entire data stack.

features.title: Collaborative tracking plan editor
features.description: A shared workspace for defining analytics events, properties, and naming conventions with branching and review workflows.

features.title: Codegen tracking libraries
features.description: Generates type-safe analytics tracking code for iOS, Android, web, and backend languages that matches the approved tracking plan.

features.title: Inspector data-quality monitoring
features.description: Compares live production event data against the tracking plan and flags missing or mismatched events and properties.

features.title: Schema sync
features.description: Pushes validated event schemas downstream into data warehouses or an organization's schema registry.

features.title: Approval workflows and branch audits
features.description: Requires review and sign-off on tracking plan changes before they merge, similar to a code review process.

features.title: Stakeholder impact visibility
features.description: Shows which dashboards, reports, and teams are affected before a tracking plan change ships.

features.title: Analytics tool integrations
features.description: Connects with platforms such as Segment, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Google Analytics, mParticle, and Rudderstack.

features.title: Enterprise governance controls
features.description: Enforceable data design standards, SSO, and admin approval requirements for large organizations.

pros: Replaces error-prone analytics spreadsheets with a purpose-built, collaborative tracking plan tool
Codegen catches analytics implementation mistakes at compile time rather than after launch
Inspector gives ongoing, automated monitoring of production event data quality
Free plan is usable for small teams without requiring a sales conversation
Backed by credible investors (GGV Capital, Heavybit) and a Y Combinator alumnus company
cons: Team plan pricing (from $250/month annually) can be a meaningful cost for very small startups
Additional editor seat pricing adds up for larger cross-functional teams
Primarily useful for teams already committed to event-based product analytics, not a general BI tool
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a custom sales quote

faqs.question: What is Avo used for?
faqs.answer: Avo is used to design, document, and enforce analytics tracking plans, replacing spreadsheets with a collaborative interface and generating type-safe tracking code.

faqs.question: How much does Avo cost?
faqs.answer: Avo has a free plan for two editors, a Team plan starting at $250/month billed annually, and a custom-priced Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: Who founded Avo?
faqs.answer: Avo was founded in 2018 by Stefania Olafsdottir, Solvi Logason, and Arni Hermann Reynisson, former product leaders at the gaming company QuizUp.

faqs.question: Does Avo integrate with Segment or Amplitude?
faqs.answer: Yes, Avo integrates with common analytics and customer data platforms including Segment, Amplitude, Mixpanel, Google Analytics, and Rudderstack.

faqs.question: What is Avo Inspector?
faqs.answer: Inspector is Avo's monitoring feature that compares live production analytics events against the approved tracking plan and flags discrepancies.

faqs.question: Is Avo only for developers?
faqs.answer: No. Avo's tracking plan editor is designed for product managers, analysts, and other non-engineers, while Codegen and Inspector serve engineering and data teams.

faqs.question: How much funding has Avo raised?
faqs.answer: Avo has raised approximately $3.15 million in total funding, including a $3 million seed round in 2020 led by GGV Capital.

faqs.question: What company size does Avo target?
faqs.answer: Avo serves teams from early-stage startups moving off spreadsheets up to large enterprises needing enforced data governance standards.

use_cases.title: Replacing spreadsheet tracking plans
use_cases.description: Product and data teams migrate their analytics event definitions from spreadsheets into Avo's collaborative, version-controlled tracking plan.

use_cases.title: Preventing broken analytics releases
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use Avo Codegen so tracking code is validated against the schema at build time, preventing bad events from shipping.

use_cases.title: Monitoring production data quality
use_cases.description: Data teams use Avo Inspector to continuously check live event data against the tracking plan and catch regressions quickly.

features: 5 editor seats included, additional editors extra
Schema sync to up to 5 downstream registries
Up to 1 million events observed in Inspector
Schema sync across entire data stack

enrichment.short_description: Avocode was a design handoff tool that let developers inspect Sketch, Photoshop, XD, and Figma files for specs, assets, and code snippets; the product was discontinued (sunset) on October 1, 2023 after its 2021 acquisition by Ceros.
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (product discontinued; formerly an independent company acquired by Ceros)
enrichment.headquarters: Formerly Prague, Czech Republic; parent company Ceros is headquartered in New York, USA
enrichment.seo_title: Avocode (2026): What Happened to This Design Handoff Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Avocode was a Sketch, Photoshop, and Figma design handoff tool. Learn its history, features, and why it was discontinued by parent company Ceros in 2023.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Avocode was a design-to-development handoff tool founded in 2015 (some sources cite 2014) in Prague, Czech Republic, by Vu Hoang Anh, Petr Brzek, and Martin Duris, a team that had previously built the popular CSS Hat Photoshop plugin together. Avocode raised roughly 1 million dollars in a seed round in 2016 and grew into a tool used by developers to inspect designs exported from Sketch, Photoshop, Adobe XD, and later Figma, extracting CSS, measurements, colors, fonts, and assets directly from design files without needing the original design application installed. It functioned similarly to competitors like Zeplin and the built-in inspect features later added natively to Figma. On October 20, 2021, design-to-code and content platform Ceros announced it had acquired Avocode, aiming to combine Avocode's design-inspection technology with Ceros's own no-code content creation tools. Avocode continued operating as a product for roughly two more years under Ceros ownership. However, Ceros subsequently decided to consolidate its product line around newer tools, and Avocode was officially sunset on October 1, 2023; the avocode.com website itself now states the product has been retired and directs former users toward other Ceros offerings such as Studio and MarkUp rather than continuing to sell or support Avocode. As of 2026, Avocode is not available for purchase, is not receiving updates, and existing accounts are not expected to be supported going forward. It is important for anyone researching Avocode today to understand it as a discontinued, historical product rather than an active SaaS tool; teams looking for equivalent functionality (design file inspection, spec extraction, and code snippet generation for handoff between designers and developers) should evaluate active alternatives such as Figma's native Dev Mode, Zeplin, or UXPin, none of which are the direct successor to Avocode despite some confusion in search results between Avocode (acquired by Ceros) and the separately owned UXPin (acquired by Xenon Partners in 2020). No current pricing exists because the product is no longer sold.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Avocode
paragraphs: Avocode was a design handoff tool that let developers open design files created in Sketch, Photoshop, Adobe XD, and Figma directly in a browser, without needing the original design application, to extract exact measurements, colors, fonts, and CSS or code snippets for implementation.

It was founded in 2015 in Prague, Czech Republic by Vu Hoang Anh, Petr Brzek, and Martin Duris, a team that had previously built the well-known CSS Hat Photoshop plugin. Avocode raised approximately 1 million dollars in seed funding in 2016 and became a recognized name in the design-to-development handoff category alongside tools like Zeplin.

Important status update: Avocode is no longer an active product. It was acquired by Ceros in October 2021, and Ceros officially sunset (discontinued) Avocode on October 1, 2023, redirecting users to other Ceros tools. This enrichment is written to reflect that historical, discontinued status accurately rather than presenting Avocode as a currently purchasable product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features (Historical)
paragraphs: While active, Avocode allowed teams to upload design files from Sketch, Photoshop, Adobe XD, and Figma and inspect them in a shared browser workspace, generating CSS, iOS, and Android code snippets for individual layers and components.

It supported pixel-precise measurement tools, color and font extraction, asset export in multiple formats and resolutions, and version history so developers could compare design iterations. Commenting and collaboration features let designers and developers discuss specific elements directly on the design file.

Avocode also offered integrations intended to fit into existing development workflows, positioning itself as a bridge between design tools that did not natively support developer handoff at the time (this was before Figma built native Dev Mode inspection into its own product).

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing and Current Availability
paragraphs: Avocode is not currently available for purchase. It was sunset by parent company Ceros on October 1, 2023, and the former avocode.com marketing site now informs visitors that the product has been retired in favor of other Ceros tools such as Studio and MarkUp.

Historical pricing (from before the shutdown) is not reliably verifiable from current public sources and is not reported here to avoid presenting outdated numbers as current. Anyone evaluating design handoff tools today should look at active alternatives rather than Avocode.

features.title: Design file inspection (historical)
features.description: Allowed developers to open Sketch, Photoshop, Adobe XD, and Figma files in-browser without the original design app installed.

features.title: CSS and code snippet generation
features.description: Generated CSS, iOS, and Android code snippets for individual design layers and components.

features.title: Pixel-precise measurements
features.description: Provided exact spacing, sizing, and alignment measurements between design elements.

features.title: Asset export
features.description: Exported images and icons in multiple formats and resolutions directly from design files.

features.title: Color and font extraction
features.description: Pulled exact color values and font specifications from uploaded design files.

features.title: Version history
features.description: Let teams compare design file versions to track changes over time.

features.title: Commenting and collaboration
features.description: Enabled designers and developers to discuss specific design elements in a shared workspace.

features.title: Multi-tool file support
features.description: Supported uploads from Sketch, Photoshop, Adobe XD, and Figma in one interface.

pros: Historically well-regarded for accurate, multi-tool design file inspection
Founding team had prior credibility from the popular CSS Hat plugin
Reduced friction between designers and developers before native handoff tools existed in Figma
cons: Discontinued: sunset by Ceros on October 1, 2023 and no longer available for purchase
No ongoing support, updates, or new account signups
Existing users must migrate to alternative tools such as Figma Dev Mode, Zeplin, or UXPin
Historical pricing and feature details are difficult to verify precisely from current sources

faqs.question: Is Avocode still available?
faqs.answer: No. Avocode was sunset by its parent company Ceros on October 1, 2023, and is no longer sold, updated, or supported.

faqs.question: What happened to Avocode?
faqs.answer: Avocode was acquired by design-to-code platform Ceros in October 2021. Roughly two years later, Ceros discontinued Avocode to focus on other products such as Studio and MarkUp.

faqs.question: Was Avocode the same company as UXPin?
faqs.answer: No. Avocode and UXPin were separate companies. UXPin was acquired by private equity firm Xenon Partners in 2020 and remains active; Avocode was acquired by Ceros in 2021 and was later discontinued.

faqs.question: Who founded Avocode?
faqs.answer: Avocode was founded in 2015 in Prague, Czech Republic by Vu Hoang Anh, Petr Brzek, and Martin Duris, the team behind the earlier CSS Hat Photoshop plugin.

faqs.question: What did Avocode do?
faqs.answer: Avocode let developers inspect design files from Sketch, Photoshop, Adobe XD, and Figma in a browser, extracting measurements, colors, fonts, assets, and code snippets for implementation.

faqs.question: What should I use instead of Avocode?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives for design handoff today include Figma's native Dev Mode, Zeplin, and UXPin, though none are official successors to Avocode.

faqs.question: Can I still access my old Avocode account or files?
faqs.answer: Public information indicates Avocode was fully sunset in October 2023; former users should check directly with Ceros for any data export or migration options, as this is not detailed on current public pages.

use_cases.title: Historical design-to-development handoff
use_cases.description: Development teams previously used Avocode to inspect designer-created files and extract implementation-ready specs and code.

use_cases.title: Cross-tool design collaboration
use_cases.description: Teams using a mix of Sketch, Photoshop, XD, or Figma previously used Avocode as a common inspection layer across file types.

use_cases.title: Migrating away from Avocode
use_cases.description: Teams that previously relied on Avocode now need to evaluate and migrate to an active alternative such as Figma Dev Mode or Zeplin.

enrichment.short_description: AwardSpace is a web hosting provider best known for its ad-free free hosting tier, alongside paid shared, semi-dedicated, and VPS cloud hosting plans.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; paid shared hosting plans from $0.25/month
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (small to mid-size hosting company)
enrichment.headquarters: Kiel, Germany (data center operations in Sofia, Bulgaria)
enrichment.seo_title: AwardSpace Review 2026: Free Web Hosting and Pricing Plans
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AwardSpace offers free, ad-free web hosting plus paid shared, semi-dedicated, and VPS plans. See 2026 pricing, features, and limits before you sign up.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AwardSpace is a web hosting provider founded in 2003, formed from the combined expertise of Zetta Hosting Solutions Ltd (a hosting company founded in 2002) and AttractSoft GmbH, a Germany-based software development company. The company is headquartered in Kiel, Germany, with data center infrastructure in Sofia, Bulgaria, and has not publicly disclosed detailed employee counts or external funding; it appears to operate as an established, privately held hosting business rather than a venture-backed startup. AwardSpace is best known in the hosting industry for pioneering ad-free free web hosting, a niche where many competitors either force advertisements onto customer websites or impose very short trial periods. Its free hosting plan includes 1GB of SSD-based storage, roughly 5GB of monthly bandwidth, support for one primary domain and up to three subdomains, PHP and MySQL support, one-click WordPress or Joomla installation, and a 15MB maximum file upload size, with no advertising injected into hosted sites. Beyond free hosting, AwardSpace sells three tiers of paid shared hosting (Basic, Web Pro Plus, and Max Pack Plus) starting around 0.25 to 0.30 dollars per month promotionally and running up to roughly 9 to 12 dollars per month regularly, all advertised with unlimited disk space and traffic, multiple domains, PHP 5/7/8 and Perl support, and a 30-day money-back guarantee. It also offers semi-dedicated hosting plans (roughly 16 to 44 dollars per month) with defined CPU and RAM allocation caps and higher MySQL query limits, and VPS cloud hosting plans (roughly 13 to 62 dollars per month) with dedicated RAM, disk, and bandwidth allocations. AwardSpace's target audience is primarily individuals, hobbyists, students, and small businesses who want free or very low-cost hosting for personal sites, blogs, or small WordPress projects, as well as slightly larger sites that need predictable semi-dedicated or VPS resources without the cost of full dedicated servers. It competes with other free and budget hosting providers such as InfinityFree, 000webhost, and Hostinger's budget tiers; its main differentiator is the no-forced-advertising policy on its free plan, which is comparatively rare in the free web hosting category.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AwardSpace
paragraphs: AwardSpace is a web hosting company founded in 2003, headquartered in Kiel, Germany, with data center operations in Sofia, Bulgaria. It grew out of a combination of Zetta Hosting Solutions and AttractSoft GmbH, and has built its reputation primarily around offering genuinely free web hosting without forcing advertisements onto customer websites.

Alongside its free tier, AwardSpace sells a full range of paid hosting products, including shared hosting, semi-dedicated hosting with defined resource caps, and VPS cloud hosting, giving customers a path to upgrade as their sites grow beyond what a free plan can support.

The company markets itself around ease of use for beginners, including one-click installers for platforms like WordPress and Joomla, alongside standard developer tools like PHP, MySQL, and PostgreSQL support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AwardSpace's free hosting plan includes 1GB of SSD storage, around 5GB of monthly bandwidth, one domain plus up to three subdomains, PHP/MySQL support, and no forced advertising, which is uncommon among free hosting providers. A 15MB maximum upload size applies to file uploads through the file manager or FTP.

Paid shared hosting plans advertise unlimited disk space and unlimited traffic, support for multiple domains (from 2 up to unlimited depending on tier), PHP 5, 7, and 8 support, Perl, and on higher tiers ASP.NET support, plus one or more free domains for life bundled into the higher-tier plans.

Semi-dedicated hosting plans allocate a defined percentage of shared server CPU and RAM (20 percent to 50 percent depending on tier) along with higher MySQL query-per-hour allowances, while VPS cloud hosting plans provide dedicated RAM (1GB to 4GB), SSD disk space (25GB to 100GB), and defined monthly traffic allowances (2TB to 8TB) with a static IP address.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AwardSpace's Free plan costs $0 and includes 1GB SSD storage and roughly 5GB monthly bandwidth with no ads. Paid Shared Hosting starts at a promotional $0.25/month (Basic plan, regularly $0.30/month) and scales up to Web Pro Plus at roughly $7.57/month and Max Pack Plus at roughly $9.83/month, all advertised with unlimited disk space and traffic.

Semi-Dedicated Hosting plans range from roughly $15.99/month (Starter) to $35.99/month (Advanced), with higher CPU/RAM allocation caps and MySQL query limits at each tier. VPS Cloud Hosting plans range from roughly $13.00/month (Starter, 1GB RAM/25GB disk) to $52.00/month (Advanced, 4GB RAM/100GB disk).

All paid plans are backed by a 30-day money-back guarantee and 24/7 support; discounted promotional rates shown on the pricing page are typically tied to longer commitment terms, with the 'regular' (renewal) price being higher.

features.title: Free ad-free hosting
features.description: 1GB SSD storage and around 5GB monthly bandwidth with PHP/MySQL support and no forced advertising.

features.title: One-click app installers
features.description: One-click WordPress and Joomla installation available on both free and paid plans.

features.title: Shared hosting tiers
features.description: Three paid shared hosting plans offering unlimited disk space and traffic with increasing domain allowances.

features.title: Semi-dedicated hosting
features.description: Plans with defined CPU and RAM allocation caps and higher MySQL query limits for busier sites.

features.title: VPS cloud hosting
features.description: Dedicated RAM, SSD disk, bandwidth, and a static IP address across three VPS tiers.

features.title: Free domain for life
features.description: Higher-tier shared and semi-dedicated plans bundle one or more free domain registrations.

features.title: Database support
features.description: MySQL and PostgreSQL database support included across hosting tiers.

features.title: 30-day money-back guarantee
features.description: Paid plans include a 30-day refund window and 24/7 customer support.

pros: One of the few free hosting providers that does not force ads onto customer websites
Free plan uses SSD storage rather than slower spinner-disk storage
Low entry price point for paid shared hosting plans
Clear upgrade path from free through shared, semi-dedicated, and VPS hosting
30-day money-back guarantee on paid plans
cons: Free plan bandwidth (around 5GB/month) is limited and unsuitable for higher-traffic sites
Advertised 'unlimited' disk space and traffic on shared plans is subject to a fair-use policy typical of budget hosts
Promotional pricing renews at a higher regular rate after the initial term
Company size and financial disclosures are limited compared to larger, publicly known hosting brands

faqs.question: Is AwardSpace really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AwardSpace offers a genuinely free hosting plan with 1GB of SSD storage and around 5GB of monthly bandwidth, without forcing advertisements onto hosted websites.

faqs.question: When was AwardSpace founded?
faqs.answer: AwardSpace was founded in 2003, combining expertise from Zetta Hosting Solutions and AttractSoft GmbH; it is headquartered in Kiel, Germany.

faqs.question: What are AwardSpace's paid hosting prices?
faqs.answer: Paid shared hosting starts around $0.25/month promotionally, semi-dedicated hosting starts around $15.99/month, and VPS cloud hosting starts around $13.00/month.

faqs.question: Does AwardSpace support WordPress?
faqs.answer: Yes, AwardSpace offers one-click WordPress and Joomla installation on both its free and paid hosting plans.

faqs.question: Where are AwardSpace's servers located?
faqs.answer: AwardSpace's data center operations are based in Sofia, Bulgaria, while the company is headquartered in Kiel, Germany.

faqs.question: Does AwardSpace offer a money-back guarantee?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid AwardSpace plans include a 30-day money-back guarantee.

faqs.question: What is the maximum upload file size on AwardSpace's free plan?
faqs.answer: The free hosting plan has a maximum upload file size of 15MB through both the file manager and FTP.

faqs.question: Does AwardSpace offer VPS hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, AwardSpace offers VPS Cloud Hosting plans ranging from roughly $13.00/month to $52.00/month with dedicated RAM, disk, and bandwidth.

use_cases.title: Hosting a personal or hobby website for free
use_cases.description: Individuals use AwardSpace's free plan to host a small personal site or blog without paying or seeing forced ads.

use_cases.title: Launching a low-cost small business site
use_cases.description: Small businesses use AwardSpace's entry-level shared hosting plans to launch a website with a WordPress installer at minimal monthly cost.

use_cases.description: Growing sites that outgrow shared hosting limits move to AwardSpace's semi-dedicated or VPS cloud plans for dedicated CPU, RAM, and bandwidth.

enrichment.short_description: AyeDot is a short-format content publishing and social sharing platform, headquartered in India, where users create and share multimedia mini-blog posts called Dots through a block-based editor.
enrichment.seo_title: AyeDot Review 2026: Short-Format Content Sharing Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AyeDot is a free short-format publishing platform for creating and sharing multimedia mini-blog posts called Dots. See features and how it works in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AyeDot is a short-format content publishing and social sharing platform accessible at ayedot.com, headquartered in India, though the exact city, founding year, funding history, and employee count are not publicly disclosed in available sources, so those specifics should not be assumed. The platform positions itself as a place for individuals to create and share exciting, informative, short-form multimedia content in a format the company calls a 'Dot,' built through what it describes as a simple, block-based Miniblog creation editor rather than a traditional long-form blogging interface. Content on AyeDot is organized into user-run Channels, and creators are given access to a Creator Room and a Channel Studio, tools for managing, editing, and customizing how their published Dots and channel presentation appear to visitors. AyeDot is accessible from desktop and mobile browsers without requiring a dedicated app, and covers a broad mix of casual, informational, and trending content topics contributed by its user base, similar in spirit to a hybrid between a micro-blogging platform and a content discovery feed. Based on publicly available information, AyeDot does not appear to charge for core usage: there is no published pricing page, subscription tier, or paid plan structure, suggesting it currently operates as a free-to-use platform, though its underlying monetization strategy (for example, advertising) is not clearly documented in public sources and should not be assumed without direct confirmation from the company. Because AyeDot is a small, niche platform with limited independent press coverage, analyst profiles, or verifiable company records compared to major social platforms, readers should treat unverifiable details such as team size, funding, or exact launch date as unknown rather than estimate them. AyeDot's closest competitive comparisons are other short-form or micro-blogging content platforms rather than any specific software category typically covered as SaaS business tooling; it is included here as a niche content-sharing product rather than a productivity, developer, or infrastructure tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AyeDot
paragraphs: AyeDot is a short-format content publishing platform where users create and share small multimedia posts, referred to on the platform as 'Dots,' through a simple block-based editor called Miniblog. It is positioned as a space for individuals to express ideas, stories, and creativity in a lightweight, easy-to-produce format rather than traditional long-form blogging.

The platform is headquartered in India, based on available public information, though many company details such as founding year, funding, and team size are not publicly documented and are not stated here to avoid presenting unverified figures as fact.

Content on AyeDot is organized around Channels, which function as a creator's personal content hub, alongside tools such as the Creator Room and Channel Studio for managing and customizing published content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AyeDot's core feature is its Miniblog editor, a block-based content creation tool designed to make publishing short multimedia posts (Dots) fast and approachable for casual creators rather than professional bloggers.
Creators get access to a Creator Room and a Channel Studio, which provide tools to manage published Dots, edit or delete content, and customize how a creator's channel is presented to visitors.

The platform is browser-accessible from desktop, tablet, and mobile devices without requiring a dedicated native app, and includes a Trends and search feature for discovering content across the platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AyeDot does not publish a pricing page, subscription tiers, or paid plan structure in publicly available sources, and appears to be free to use for creating and publishing content.

Because no monetization details (such as advertising, creator payouts, or premium features) are clearly documented publicly, this should be treated as an open question rather than a confirmed free-forever business model.

cons: Limited public information about the company, team, and founding history

enrichment.short_description: Back4App is a backend-as-a-service (BaaS) platform built on the open-source Parse Platform, giving developers a hosted database, REST and GraphQL APIs, cloud functions, and authentication without managing server infrastructure.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; paid plans from $15/month per app
enrichment.seo_title: Back4App Review 2026: Parse-Based Backend as a Service
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Back4App is a backend-as-a-service platform built on Parse Platform, offering hosted databases, APIs, and cloud code. Compare 2026 pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Back4App is a backend-as-a-service (BaaS) platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, that provides hosted infrastructure for the open-source Parse Platform, the backend framework originally created by Parse Inc. before Facebook acquired and later shut down its own hosted Parse service in 2017. Back4App stepped in as one of the main hosting providers for Parse Server, letting developers who build mobile and web apps get a managed database (built on PostgreSQL), REST and GraphQL APIs, user authentication, push notifications, cloud functions (Cloud Code), and file storage without operating their own servers. Public reporting indicates Back4App has grown largely as a bootstrapped, self-funded company, reporting around 1.7 million dollars in annual revenue and roughly 55 million end-user records or customers served as of 2024 according to third-party business data sources, without relying on large venture capital rounds; the company reports approximately 15 to 50 employees depending on the data source. Beyond its core Backend-as-a-Service product, Back4App has expanded into a Containers offering (for deploying containerized applications and static sites) and an AI Agent feature aimed at helping developers scaffold backend infrastructure using natural-language prompts. Pricing follows a freemium, usage-based tiered model: a Free plan supports prototyping with 25,000 requests per month, 250MB of database storage, and 1GB of file storage; the MVP plan starts at 15 dollars per month per app (with annual billing discounts) for small production apps; a Pay As You Go plan starts at 80 dollars per month per app for higher-traffic applications with compliance features like SOC 2 and ISO 27001; a Dedicated plan starts at 400 dollars per month per app for dedicated infrastructure and HIPAA support; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan adds on-premises deployment and dedicated engineering support. Back4App's target audience is primarily individual developers, startups, and small-to-mid-size engineering teams who want Parse Platform's data model and open-source portability without operating their own servers, differentiating itself from proprietary BaaS platforms like Firebase or Supabase by being built on an open-source, vendor-portable framework that customers can self-host if they choose to leave the Back4App platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Back4App
paragraphs: Back4App is a backend-as-a-service platform, founded in 2015 and headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, built around the open-source Parse Platform. When Facebook shut down its hosted Parse service in 2017, Back4App became one of the main companies offering managed hosting for Parse Server, letting developers keep using the open-source Parse data model and SDKs without operating their own infrastructure.

The platform gives mobile and web developers a managed PostgreSQL-backed database, REST and GraphQL APIs, user authentication, push notifications, cloud functions, and file storage out of the box, aimed at cutting the time needed to stand up backend infrastructure for a new app.

Back4App has grown as a largely bootstrapped, self-funded company rather than through large venture rounds, and has expanded beyond core BaaS hosting into containerized app deployment and AI-assisted backend scaffolding tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Back4App's core backend platform provides a hosted database with REST and GraphQL API access, real-time queries, and Cloud Code for running custom server-side logic and scheduled jobs, all built on the open-source Parse Platform so applications remain portable and can be self-hosted if needed.

Built-in user authentication and management, push notification support, and file storage cover common app backend requirements without needing separate third-party services. Daily automated backups are included even on the free tier, with longer retention on paid plans.

Back4App Containers extends the platform to deploying containerized applications and static sites, while a newer AI Agent feature lets developers describe backend requirements in natural language to help scaffold cloud architecture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Back4App's Free plan supports prototyping with 25,000 requests per month, 250MB database storage, 1GB data transfer, and 1GB file storage, with no credit card required. The MVP plan starts at $15/month per app (with annual billing offering a discount) and raises limits to 500K requests/month and 50GB file storage.

The Pay As You Go plan starts at $80/month per app, supporting up to 5M requests/month, 250GB file storage, and compliance features like SOC 2 and ISO 27001, while the Dedicated plan starts at $400/month per app with dedicated infrastructure, unlimited requests, and HIPAA support.

An Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with on-premises deployment, dedicated engineering support, and multi-region availability. Overage charges apply on all metered plans for extra storage, transfer, and requests beyond plan limits.

features.title: Hosted Parse Platform database
features.description: A managed, PostgreSQL-backed database with REST and GraphQL API access built on the open-source Parse data model.

features.description: Server-side JavaScript functions and scheduled jobs for running custom backend logic without managing servers.

features.description: Built-in user account management and authentication for mobile and web apps.

features.description: Native push notification support for iOS and Android apps built on the platform.

features.title: File storage
features.description: Hosted file storage for app assets, images, and user-uploaded content.

features.title: Back4App Containers
features.description: Deploys containerized applications and static sites alongside the core BaaS product.

features.title: AI Agent for backend scaffolding
features.description: Generates cloud backend architecture from natural-language prompts.

features.title: Open-source portability
features.description: Built on the open-source Parse Platform, allowing apps to be self-hosted or migrated away from Back4App if needed.

pros: Built on open-source Parse Platform, avoiding vendor lock-in compared to proprietary BaaS platforms
Free tier is usable for real prototyping with no credit card required
Predictable, published tiered pricing rather than only usage-based billing
Daily automated backups included even on the free plan
Expanding beyond core BaaS into containers and AI-assisted backend generation
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community compared to Firebase or Supabase
Free and MVP plan storage and request limits are modest for growing apps
Advanced compliance features (SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA) are gated behind higher-cost plans
Smaller company size may mean less dedicated enterprise support compared to larger BaaS vendors

faqs.question: What is Back4App used for?
faqs.answer: Back4App is used as a backend-as-a-service platform, giving developers a hosted database, APIs, authentication, and cloud functions built on the open-source Parse Platform.

faqs.question: How much does Back4App cost?
faqs.answer: Back4App has a free plan for prototyping, then paid plans starting at $15/month (MVP), $80/month (Pay As You Go), and $400/month (Dedicated), plus custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Is Back4App built on Parse?
faqs.answer: Yes, Back4App is a managed hosting provider for the open-source Parse Platform, which was originally created by Parse Inc. before Facebook's acquisition and later shutdown of its hosted service in 2017.

faqs.question: When was Back4App founded?
faqs.answer: Back4App was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in Sunnyvale, California.

faqs.question: Is Back4App open source?
faqs.answer: Back4App is a hosted commercial service, but it is built on the open-source Parse Platform, meaning applications can be self-hosted or migrated away if needed.

faqs.question: Does Back4App support GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, Back4App provides both REST and GraphQL API access to its hosted database.

faqs.question: Who is Back4App for?
faqs.answer: Back4App targets individual developers, startups, and small-to-mid-size engineering teams building mobile and web apps who want a managed backend without running their own servers.

faqs.question: Does Back4App have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Back4App's free plan includes 25,000 requests/month, 250MB database storage, and 1GB file storage with no credit card required.

use_cases.title: Backend for mobile apps

enrichment.short_description: Backblaze (NASDAQ: BLZE) is a publicly traded cloud storage company offering B2 Cloud Storage, an S3-compatible object storage service for developers and businesses, alongside its original unlimited Personal Backup product for consumers.
enrichment.starting_price: B2 Cloud Storage from $6.95/TB per month; Personal Backup from $7/month per computer
enrichment.company_size: 380+ employees (per most recent public SEC filings)
enrichment.seo_title: Backblaze Review 2026: B2 Cloud Storage and Backup Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Backblaze (NASDAQ: BLZE) offers B2 Cloud Storage and unlimited Personal Backup. Compare 2026 pricing, features, and how it compares to AWS S3 and Dropbox.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Backblaze, Inc. is a publicly traded cloud storage company (NASDAQ: BLZE) founded in 2007 by Gleb Budman along with several co-founders including Brian Wilson, and headquartered in San Mateo, California. Backblaze is notable for having grown for over a decade as a bootstrapped, capital-efficient company, raising less than 3 million dollars in venture funding before its November 2021 IPO on the Nasdaq exchange. As of its most recent public SEC filings, the company reported roughly 380 full-time employees. Backblaze operates two main product lines. Its original product, Personal Backup (part of its Computer Backup line, which also includes Business Backup), is a consumer and small-business subscription service that provides unlimited automatic backup of an entire computer's data, with pricing historically around 7 dollars per month, 70 dollars per year, or 130 dollars for two years per computer, along with features like ransomware protection assistance and remote file access. Its second, faster-growing product line is B2 Cloud Storage, an S3-API-compatible object storage service positioned as a lower-cost alternative to Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, and Azure Blob Storage, used for application development, media asset storage, backup targets, and AI/ML data pipelines. B2 pricing is usage-based: pay-as-you-go storage costs 6.95 dollars per terabyte per month with the first 10GB always free and free egress (data downloaded out of Backblaze) up to three times the average monthly storage stored, beyond which egress costs 0.01 dollars per GB; B2 Reserve offers annual capacity-based bundles starting around 19,500 dollars for 250TB per year (about 78 dollars per TB per year) with a 20TB minimum commitment; and B2 Overdrive, aimed at multi-petabyte AI and high-performance computing workloads, starts around 15 dollars per TB per month with unlimited free egress. Backblaze reports serving over 500,000 customers across roughly 175 countries. Its target audience spans individual and small-business consumers needing simple unlimited backup, and developers, media companies, and enterprises needing affordable, S3-compatible object storage as an alternative to hyperscaler cloud storage pricing, particularly its free/low-cost egress positioning versus AWS S3. As a public company, Backblaze's financial results (revenue, growth rate, and profitability) are disclosed quarterly via SEC filings and are subject to change over time; readers evaluating current financial performance should consult Backblaze's most recent investor relations filings rather than relying on any single snapshot.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Backblaze
paragraphs: Backblaze is a publicly traded cloud storage company, listed on the Nasdaq under the ticker BLZE since its November 2021 IPO. Founded in 2007 by Gleb Budman and co-founders including Brian Wilson, and headquartered in San Mateo, California, Backblaze built its reputation as a capital-efficient, largely bootstrapped company that grew for years on very little outside venture funding before going public.

The company operates two core product lines: Computer Backup (including Personal Backup and Business Backup), its original consumer and small-business unlimited backup subscription, and B2 Cloud Storage, an S3-API-compatible object storage service aimed at developers, media companies, and enterprises as a lower-cost alternative to hyperscaler cloud storage.

Backblaze reports serving more than 500,000 customers across roughly 175 countries, spanning individual consumers backing up personal computers to businesses storing application data, media libraries, and AI training datasets in B2.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Personal Backup provides unlimited, automatic backup of an entire computer, including external drives, with continuous background upload, versioned file history, ransomware recovery assistance, and the ability to access or restore files remotely from any device.

B2 Cloud Storage provides S3-API-compatible object storage, meaning many tools and applications built for Amazon S3 can connect to B2 with minimal changes. It supports use cases like backup and archive targets, media asset storage and delivery, application data storage, and AI/ML training data pipelines.

A key differentiator for B2 is its egress pricing: data downloaded out of B2 is free up to three times the average amount of data stored, which Backblaze positions as dramatically cheaper than typical hyperscaler egress fees, alongside partnerships with CDN providers to extend free egress further for content delivery use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Personal Backup pricing has historically been structured around $7/month, $70/year, or $130 for a two-year term per computer, for unlimited backup storage of one computer's data.

B2 Cloud Storage pay-as-you-go pricing is $6.95/TB per month for storage, with the first 10GB always free, no minimum file size or duration fees, and free egress up to 3x the average monthly storage stored (then $0.01/GB beyond that).

For larger, predictable workloads, B2 Reserve offers annual capacity bundles starting at roughly $19,500 for 250TB per year (about $78/TB/year, 20TB minimum commitment, 1-5 year terms), and B2 Overdrive offers multi-petabyte-scale pricing starting around $15/TB per month with unlimited free egress for high-throughput AI and HPC workloads.

features.description: Predictable annual capacity-based pricing bundles for large, steady storage workloads.

features.title: B2 Overdrive for AI/HPC workloads

pros: Publicly traded (NASDAQ: BLZE) with transparent, audited quarterly financial disclosures

Personal Backup offers genuinely unlimited storage per computer at a flat price, unlike many competitors with storage caps
S3-API compatibility makes B2 easy to adopt for teams already using AWS S3 tooling
Long operating history (founded 2007) with a strong reputation for reliability and published hard drive reliability data
cons: Personal Backup covers one computer per subscription, not unlimited devices
B2 Reserve and Overdrive require significant minimum commitments (20TB and multi-petabyte respectively), less suited to small workloads
As a smaller public company, Backblaze has fewer global data center regions than AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure
Egress beyond the free allowance still incurs per-GB charges on pay-as-you-go B2 plans

faqs.question: Is Backblaze publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, Backblaze, Inc. trades on the Nasdaq under the ticker symbol BLZE, having completed its IPO in November 2021.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Backblaze Personal Backup and B2 Cloud Storage?
faqs.answer: Personal Backup is a consumer subscription for unlimited automatic backup of one computer, while B2 Cloud Storage is an S3-compatible object storage service for developers and businesses, priced by usage.

faqs.question: How much does Backblaze B2 cost?
faqs.answer: B2 pay-as-you-go pricing is $6.95/TB per month for storage, with free egress up to 3x average monthly storage stored and $0.01/GB beyond that.

faqs.question: How much does Backblaze Personal Backup cost?
faqs.answer: Personal Backup has historically been priced around $7/month, $70/year, or $130 for two years per computer, for unlimited storage of one computer's data.

faqs.question: When was Backblaze founded?
faqs.answer: Backblaze was founded in 2007 by Gleb Budman and co-founders including Brian Wilson, and is headquartered in San Mateo, California.

faqs.question: Is B2 Cloud Storage compatible with AWS S3?
faqs.answer: Yes, B2 Cloud Storage offers an S3-compatible API, allowing many tools and applications built for Amazon S3 to connect to B2 with minimal changes.

faqs.question: How many customers does Backblaze have?
faqs.answer: Backblaze reports serving more than 500,000 customers across roughly 175 countries.

faqs.question: What is B2 Reserve?
faqs.answer: B2 Reserve is Backblaze's annual, capacity-based pricing bundle for large, predictable storage workloads, starting around $19,500 for 250TB per year with a 20TB minimum commitment.

use_cases.title: Lower-cost object storage for applications

enrichment.short_description: Backendless is a visual backend-as-a-service and no-code app development platform offering a managed database, cloud code, real-time features, and a drag-and-drop UI Builder for building mobile and web applications.
enrichment.seo_title: Backendless Review 2026: Visual No-Code Backend Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Backendless is a visual backend-as-a-service and no-code app builder with a managed database, cloud code, and UI Builder. See 2026 pricing and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Backendless is a visual backend-as-a-service (BaaS) and no-code/low-code application development platform founded in 2012 by Mark Piller, who continues to serve as its founder and CEO. The company is headquartered in Frisco, Texas, and reports an estimated 25 to 50 employees, operating as a privately held company. Backendless is designed to let developers, and to a significant extent non-developers, build the backend and, through its visual UI Builder, the frontend of mobile and web applications without writing extensive custom server code. Core capabilities include a managed, scalable database with REST and other API access, visual Cloud Code for business logic, real-time database and messaging via WebSockets, user authentication and management, push notifications, geolocation services, and file storage. A distinguishing feature relative to many BaaS competitors like Firebase or Supabase is Backendless's drag-and-drop UI Builder, which lets teams visually construct application interfaces bound directly to backend data, positioning the product closer to a full no-code app development platform than a backend-only service. Backendless offers three main pricing paths: Backendless Cloud, a free-to-paid managed cloud offering aimed at individuals and small businesses, with a free plan (50 API requests per minute, 20 database tables, 1GB file storage) and paid Scale plans starting around 15 dollars per month that scale with usage (offered in both variable, usage-metered and fixed, tier-based pricing structures) and unlock unlimited API requests, more database tables, and higher file storage and real-time connection limits; Backendless Pro, a self-hosted, on-premises deployment option marketed with free licensing for core functionality aimed at medium-to-large enterprises that need to run Backendless inside their own infrastructure; and Managed Backendless, a premium enterprise service that adds 24x7x365 monitoring plus dedicated server administration and DevOps support on top of a self-hosted deployment. All Backendless Cloud plans include unlimited developer seats at no extra charge, differentiating its pricing model from many competitors that charge per developer seat. Backendless's target audience spans solo and indie developers prototyping apps, startups needing to ship quickly without a dedicated backend team, and enterprises wanting either a fully managed cloud backend or a self-hosted deployment for data residency or compliance reasons. It competes most directly with Firebase, Supabase, and AWS Amplify, differentiating primarily through its visual, no-code UI Builder and its self-hosted Pro deployment option, which many purely cloud-native BaaS competitors do not offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Backendless
paragraphs: Backendless is a visual backend-as-a-service and no-code application development platform founded in 2012 by Mark Piller. Headquartered in Frisco, Texas, the company positions itself as more than a typical BaaS: alongside a managed database, cloud functions, and real-time features, it offers a drag-and-drop UI Builder for constructing application interfaces bound to backend data.

The platform is aimed at helping both professional developers and less technical builders ship mobile and web apps faster by handling common backend infrastructure (database, authentication, file storage, real-time messaging) out of the box, while still allowing custom logic through Cloud Code.

Backendless offers both a fully managed cloud deployment (Backendless Cloud) and a self-hosted deployment option (Backendless Pro), which is less common among mainstream BaaS competitors and appeals to organizations with data residency or compliance requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Backendless provides a managed, scalable database with REST API access, alongside visual Cloud Code tools for defining custom business logic without necessarily writing traditional server code line by line, though code-based development is also supported.

Real-time capabilities let apps sync data instantly across connected clients via WebSocket-based messaging and live database updates, while built-in user authentication, geolocation services, push notifications, and file storage cover common app infrastructure needs.

The UI Builder is Backendless's most distinctive feature: a drag-and-drop visual editor for building application front ends that bind directly to backend data models, letting teams prototype and ship functional app interfaces without hand-coding every screen.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Backendless Cloud's Free plan includes 50 API requests per minute, 20 database tables, 1GB file storage, 10 cached objects, 5 real-time connections, and 2 cloud code scripts, aimed at exploring and learning the platform at no cost.

The Scale plan starts around $15 per month and scales with usage, offered in Scale Variable (daily charges based on peak API request volume) and Scale Fixed (a set monthly price by usage tier) variations, unlocking unlimited API requests, more database tables, larger file storage, more real-time connections, and daily backups. All Backendless Cloud plans include unlimited developer seats at no extra cost.

Backendless Pro is a self-hosted, on-premises deployment aimed at medium-to-large enterprises, marketed with free core licensing, while Managed Backendless is a premium enterprise service adding 24x7x365 monitoring and dedicated server administration and DevOps support on top of a self-hosted deployment.

features.title: Managed database with REST API
features.description: A scalable, hosted database with REST API access for storing and querying application data.

features.title: Visual Cloud Code
features.description: Tools for defining custom backend business logic visually or through code, without managing servers.

features.title: Drag-and-drop UI Builder
features.description: A visual editor for building app front ends bound directly to backend data, without hand-coding every screen.

features.title: Real-time data and messaging
features.description: WebSocket-based real-time database updates and pub/sub messaging for live app features.

features.title: User authentication and management
features.description: Built-in account creation, login, and user management for mobile and web apps.

features.title: Push notifications
features.description: Native push notification support integrated with the backend platform.

features.title: Geolocation services
features.description: Built-in geolocation features for location-aware application functionality.

features.title: Self-hosted Backendless Pro deployment
features.description: An on-premises deployment option for enterprises with data residency or compliance requirements.

pros: Visual UI Builder extends the platform beyond backend-only services into no-code app development
Unlimited developer seats included on all Backendless Cloud plans at no extra cost
Offers both fully managed cloud and self-hosted (Pro) deployment options
Free plan is usable for learning and small projects without a credit card
Combines database, cloud code, real-time, auth, and UI building in a single platform
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community compared to Firebase or Supabase
Scale plan usage-based pricing can be harder to predict than flat-tier competitors
Free plan limits (50 requests/minute, 20 tables) are restrictive for anything beyond prototyping
Self-hosted Backendless Pro requires more infrastructure and DevOps expertise to operate

faqs.question: What is Backendless used for?
faqs.answer: Backendless is used as a visual backend-as-a-service and no-code platform for building the database, business logic, and front-end interfaces of mobile and web applications.

faqs.question: How much does Backendless cost?
faqs.answer: Backendless Cloud has a free plan, with Scale plans starting around $15/month that scale with usage; Backendless Pro (self-hosted) offers free core licensing, and Managed Backendless is a custom-priced enterprise service.

faqs.question: Who founded Backendless?
faqs.answer: Backendless was founded in 2012 by Mark Piller, who remains the company's founder and CEO; it is headquartered in Frisco, Texas.

faqs.question: Does Backendless support self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, Backendless Pro is a self-hosted, on-premises deployment option aimed at enterprises needing data residency or compliance control.

faqs.question: What is Backendless UI Builder?
faqs.answer: UI Builder is a drag-and-drop visual editor for building application front ends bound directly to Backendless backend data, without hand-coding every screen.

faqs.question: Does Backendless charge per developer seat?
faqs.answer: No, all Backendless Cloud plans include unlimited developer seats at no additional cost.

faqs.question: How does Backendless compare to Firebase?
faqs.answer: Backendless competes directly with Firebase and Supabase but differentiates with its visual UI Builder for no-code app interfaces and a self-hosted Pro deployment option that Firebase does not offer.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Backendless?
faqs.answer: Yes, Backendless Cloud's free plan includes 50 API requests per minute, 20 database tables, and 1GB of file storage.

use_cases.title: Rapid mobile app backend development
use_cases.description: Developers use Backendless's managed database, authentication, and push notifications to build mobile app backends quickly.

use_cases.title: No-code app prototyping
use_cases.description: Non-technical builders use Backendless's UI Builder combined with its backend to prototype functional app interfaces without writing code.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise deployment
use_cases.description: Enterprises with data residency or compliance requirements deploy Backendless Pro on their own infrastructure instead of using the managed cloud.
